element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Player establishes initial routes and airport slots
Player must establish presence in all geographic regions
Player competes with 3 AI/human opponents for regional passenger supremacy
Player must maintain positive annual profit margin
Player learns to match aircraft capabilities to route demands and regional aircraft availability
Player optimizes price relative to competition and demand
Player navigates Cold War era restrictions (1955-1986); Western vs Eastern Bloc airplane availability

element_introduction_order.first_appears: Early game years (varies by era selected)
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Player adapts strategy to geopolitical events (oil crises, wars, EU expansion, Berlin Wall fall)
Sudden shift in aircraft availability and pricing as Eastern Bloc access improves
Player must avoid going bankrupt; simultaneous bankruptcy = all-lose condition
Player adapts to new aircraft tech that quickly becomes obsolete by 2016
Nations request airline funding for alternative fuel research; volcanic/weather events

typical_tension_triggers: Historical crisis event (oil crisis 1973, Iran-Iraq War, etc.)
Competitor airline dominance in target region
Cold War restrictions limiting aircraft options
Stalemate risk (no airline meeting 20-year victory conditions)
rhythm_pattern.pacing_descriptor: Turn-based annual cycle with event-driven tension spikes. Steady pressure to meet three concurrent objectives (regional hubs, passenger dominance, profitability) escalates as 20-year time window closes. Crisis events create sudden disruption requiring strategic pivot.

spatial_grammar.optional_content_signal: Regional hub placement is mandatory; optional depth involves secondary route expansion beyond minimum requirements

difficulty_curve.early_game: Moderate: Player establishes first routes and learns profitability/hub mechanics. Limited geopolitical complications in first few years depending on era.
difficulty_curve.mid_game: Rising: Historical events accumulate (oil crises, wars). Cold War era includes aircraft access restrictions requiring strategic pivot. Competitor airlines establish positions.
difficulty_curve.late_game: High: 20-year victory window closes; must achieve all three concurrent win conditions. Regional dominance increasingly contested. Post-1986/post-2000 geopolitical shifts force aircraft fleet overhauls.
notable_spikes: 1973 oil crisis (1955-1990 era) - profitability pressure spike
1986 Perestroika in Cold War eras - aircraft market disruption and pricing collapse
1989-1991 Eastern Bloc collapse - further cost reduction spike
2007-2016 supersonic aircraft lifecycle - rapid obsolescence pressure
Years 18-20 - critical win condition window with no margin for error

notable_level_patterns.description: Game presents 4 distinct 20-year play spans (1955-1975, 1970-1990, 1985-2005, 2000-2020) with historically-informed event scheduling. Each era introduces different strategic constraints (jet dawn, oil instability, economic prosperity, supersonic transition).
notable_level_patterns.example_level: 1970-1990 era includes both oil crisis (1973) and Cold War thaw (1986), forcing two major strategic pivots

notable_level_patterns.description: Player must simultaneously achieve: regional hub presence (all regions), passenger dominance in 4-7 regions (difficulty-dependent), annual profitability, AND survive 20 years without ties/draws. No single objective can be abandoned.
notable_level_patterns.example_level: Mid-game year 10-15: Eastern Bloc airline must balance cheap aircraft cost advantage against Western Bloc aircraft quality superiority after perestroika opens access

notable_level_patterns.description: Historical events (Suez Crisis, Yom Kippur War, Iran-Iraq War, EU expansion, Berlin Wall) create chained disruptions. Cold War-era divisions persist until 1986 when normalization shifts aircraft market. Later eras introduce hypothetical events (EU expansion to Russia, climate crises).
notable_level_patterns.example_level: 1955-1975 era: Player experiences sequential events (1959 Castro rise, 1966 Cultural Revolution impact, 1973 oil crisis) requiring continuous strategy adjustment
